item.title: Re: Shouldn’t this be Keter?
item.content:encoded:
It's Euclid because the Foundation can't comprehend it enough to respond to it or take it seriously. They literally can't conceive of the idea that Big Bird is killing these people, so it just seems like a weird infohazard that appears in some cases of natural and accidental death.

Plus because of the antimeme, it can't pose a threat to normalcy.

item.title: Shouldn’t this be Keter?
item.content:encoded: Nice concept, though I fail to understand why this isn’t Keter. Seems to be completely uncontained and mostly random except for a few factors (which the Foundation barely comprehend).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Necroposting, but I’m pretty sure “EndingQ” is making a joke here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If there was some report about what they were researching before they met their fate, it would open up some ideas as to what they stumbled upon without giving too much away. It's just barely on the "too vague" side of mystery for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of there being some mathematical abnormality that is triggering this entity.
One small qualm -

Automated systems designed to detect SCP-2109 related discrepancies in legal, medical, press and bureaucratic documents are to be used to detect potential SCP-2109 cases.

This sentence appears to be saying the same thing twice, in regards to the detection of SCP-2109 cases.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bird-man who no-one can see, stalking and killing mathematicians for something they discovered, and then going on to anomalously conceal the murders? Amazing.

Seriously, good work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did you read the entire skip?

The last addenda mentions an unknown humanoid entity that no one can see in person.

If you didn't read the whole thing, I suggest you do before downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn't understand how this is an SCP. It just seems like a coincidental story to me. I didn't like it. No offense to the author. Downvoted for this reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good catch regarding the date of the disappearance. However, I'm intentionally using day/month/year notation rather than the American-style notation because I'm a rampant nationalist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dates written with slashes should be month/day/year, not day/month/year- July 21 2008, for instance, is 07/21/2008.

Yes, if one assumes everything is written using the American date notation. In reality, using dd/mm/yyyy is fine. If anything, one would assume The Foundation, adhering to worldwide standards, would adopt the ISO format (yyyy/mm/dd). Please think your feedback through before you post it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dates written with slashes should be month/day/year, not day/month/year- July 21 2008, for instance, is 07/21/2008.
Also, the date the entity followed Dr. C home is probably wrong- the entity's first appearance on tape is noted as "28/02/2010" (February 28, I presume), and it's described following people around for the next two days, but then it gets into Dr. C's car on "02/02/2010" (February 2) and coming back with her head on "03/03/2010" (March 3). I'm guessing the intended date of departure is March 2.

Otherwise- good stuff, I like it. We may know what the thing looks like now, but we still have no idea what it wants, what the symbols mean, why its methods vary, or whether the one we've seen is the only one… and creepiest of all, we have no plan for figuring any of this out, because we can't observe it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, all Foundation sites should probably use SILVER CRESCENT to analyze their security footage, not just for this but for all the other infohazardous crap that shows up.
Nevertheless, pretty creepy and a good example of an indirectly observed, barely containable SCP. Though the robe guy is getting pretty blatant if he thinks the Foundation of all people will write off a severed head appearing in a cabinet as "natural" or "accidental." Then again, maybe it realized they were onto it and decided to demonstrate that they currently have no way to stop it.
Regardless, +1.
(Though shouldn't the cloaked guy be designated SCP-2109-1? It reminds me of the guy from SCP-1981…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apartments in the UK are called flats

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was pretty creeped out until the very end - the idea that these mathematicians were discovering something dangerous, learning some mathematical truth, some proof or formula that… SOMETHING doesn't want them to know… That's pretty creepy. The bird-monk-thing kind of spoiled the mystery for me. Still worth an upvote, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't really like the reveal that the murders were being perpetrated by super-ominous-monk-robe-dudes. Other than that, I'm digging this, especially the part where the Foundation is affected by the anomaly in how they suspect that the deaths may indeed have been natural. With most mind-affecting skips, the researcher who ostensibly writes these case files up usually appear to be magically immune to them for sake of ease of reading, but I'm glad you didn't take that route. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty good. Due to my intense hatred of math, I get a kick out of the fact that there is some kind of eldritch abomination from another dimension (or what have you) that hates math enough to murder mathematicians. Take my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brilliant - although I liked it a lot more until the description of the entity, and would have preferred it to end immediately after the table. I guess that these days any kind of monster reveal is less creepy to me than absolutely none at all.

Nevertheless, I love the idea of an SCP that we simply can't detect by ourselves no matter what we do. How many other SCPs are out there - or even in the real world - that we just simply have no way of perceiving?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, I just noticed they're all mathematicians and math enthusiasts. -i2 additional votes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I was worried when I was writing it that people were going to say it wasn't anomalous enough for the site. I mean, technically speaking it could all be a tragic coincidence, but I think the Foundation is paranoid enough to document it anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno, man. Those deaths all seem pretty natural to me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I initially decided not to tag this, because I was unsure of which tags qualified, but for efficiency's sake I've added tags now. If anyone more suited to tagging than I would read this over and make sure I've not screwed the tags up, that would be appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one took me a bit to warm up to, and some tinkering might help push it from weird and moderately creepy to disturbing, but I have to +1 for a well developed bizarre idea, and the easy to miss "we have no idea how to lock this one up, so we're just going to cover it up when it happens".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After a year with no usable ideas, I've written up two things in ten days. Whether or not that's a good thing is up to the audience to decide. Inspired by a news snippet from way, way back that popped into my head, and my undying hatred of maths.

Sincere thanks to Tuomey, Athena Grey and a chat user whose name escapes me for looking over it in chat yesterday.
